title: Antiemetics
body_html:
The antiemetics are used to treat nausea and vomiting (N&V). If antiemetic drug treatment is indicated, drug choice is guided by the aetiology of nausea and vomiting.

The mechanism of action of some of the commonly used antiemetics is summarised in the table below:

AntiemeticMode of Action
CyclizineHistamine H1-receptor antagonist
PromethazineHistamine H1-receptor antagonist
OndansetronSerotonin 5-HT3 receptor antagonist
ProchlorperazineDopamine D2 receptor antagonist
MetoclopramideDopamine D2 receptor antagonist
5-HT3 receptor antagonist
5-HT4 receptor agonist

Antiemetics may be grouped into classes. These are shown below with type examples and common indications:

ClassExamplesConditionsNotes
AntihistaminesCinnarizine
Cyclizine
Promethazine		Several conditions e.g.
motion sickness,
Post-op N&V caused by opioids or GA		Adverse effects, e.g. drowsiness and antimuscarinic effects, differ between antihistamines
PhenothiazinesChlorpromazine
Prochlorperazine
Trifluoperazine		Chemotherapy-induced N&V

Radiation-induced N&V

Post-op N&V caused by opioids or GA		Act centrally by blocking the chemoreceptor trigger zone.
Severe dystonic reactions sometimes occur (especially in children).
Prochlorperazine available as a buccal tablet.
Other antipsychoticHaloperidol
Levomepromazine		N&V in palliative care
Antiemetic & prokineticMetoclopramideSuperior to phenothiazines for emesis associated with gastro-intestinal and biliary diseaseAssociated with acute dystonic reactions
Limit prescribing to no longer than 5 days
Dopamine antagonistDomperidoneParkinson’s diseaseActs at the chemoreceptor trigger zone.

Less likely to cause central effects as does not readily cross the blood-brain barrier
5HT3-receptor antagonistsGranisetron
Ondansetron
Palonosetron		N&V in patients receiving cytotoxicsCommon side effects include:
  • constipation
  • feeling hot
  • headache
  • abnormal sensation
